coredata.dc:title: Copper and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as green nano-probes for fluorimetric determination of delafloxacin; characterization and applications

coredata.dc:description:
Background Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained much interest recently for being efficient probes. Their cost-effectiveness, eco-friendliness, and unique photocatalytic activities made them distinctive alternatives to other luminescent approaches like fluorescent dyes and luminous derivatization. Meanwhile, delafloxacin (DLF) is a recently approved antibacterial medicine. DLF has been authorized for the treatment of soft-tissue and skin infections as well as pneumonia. Therefore, new eco-friendly, cost-effective, and sensitive tools are needed its estimation in different matrices.
Results In the proposed study, green copper and nitrogen carbon dots (Cu-N@CDs) were synthesized from a green source (plum juice with copper sulphate). Cu-N@CQDs were then characterized using multiple tools including X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS), FTIR and UV-VIS spectroscopy, Zeta potential measurements, High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and fluorescence spectroscopy. After gradually adding DLF, the developed quantum dots’ fluorescence was significantly enhanced within the working range of 0.5–100.0 ng mL−1. The limits of detection and quantification were 0.08 and 0.27 ng mL−1, respectively. The accuracy of the proposed method ranged from 96.00 to 99.12 % in recovery%, when recovered from milk and plasma samples.
Significance Cu-N@CDs were utilized and validated for selectively determining DLF in several matrices including pharmaceutical forms, human plasma and in milk samples using spectrofluorimetric technique. The bio-analytical method is simple and could be used in content uniformity testing as well as in therapeutic drug monitoring in human plasma.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained much interest recently for being efficient probes. Their cost-effectiveness, eco-friendliness, and unique photocatalytic activities made them distinctive alternatives to other luminescent approaches like fluorescent dyes and luminous derivatization. Meanwhile, delafloxacin (DLF) is a recently approved antibacterial medicine. DLF has been authorized for the treatment of soft-tissue and skin infections as well as pneumonia. Therefore, new eco-friendly, cost-effective, and sensitive tools are needed its estimation in different matrices.

In the proposed study, green copper and nitrogen carbon dots (Cu-N@CDs) were synthesized from a green source (plum juice with copper sulphate). Cu-N@CQDs were then characterized using multiple tools including X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS), FTIR and UV-VIS spectroscopy, Zeta potential measurements, High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and fluorescence spectroscopy. After gradually adding DLF, the developed quantum dots’ fluorescence was significantly enhanced within the working range of 0.5–100.0 ng mL−1. The limits of detection and quantification were 0.08 and 0.27 ng mL−1, respectively. The accuracy of the proposed method ranged from 96.00 to 99.12 % in recovery%, when recovered from milk and plasma samples.

Cu-N@CDs were utilized and validated for selectively determining DLF in several matrices including pharmaceutical forms, human plasma and in milk samples using spectrofluorimetric technique. The bio-analytical method is simple and could be used in content uniformity testing as well as in therapeutic drug monitoring in human plasma.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the high spread of the antibiotic resistance, the development and creation of new molecules with enhanced susceptibilities is necessary [1]. The United Nations (UN) declared that without development of new antibacterial entities, the total global mortalities could rise, by the year 2050, to 10 million of human deaths per year [2]. Delafloxacin has been developed and recently approved by the US-FDA as fluoroquinolone with a unique non-zwitterionic chemical structure [3]. Delafloxacin has the capability of suppressing topoisomerases II and IV simultaneously, which are important for the activity of bacteria. This in turn broaden its activity spectrum against clinically resistant pathogens [4,5]. Delafloxacin is formulated as delafloxacin meglumine salt (DLF, chemical structure Fig. 1 a). DLF was approved for the treatment of community-acquired pneumonia, which is one of the most substantial causes of death amongst bacterial infections worldwide. It's also one of the most effective antibacterial agents against skin and skin structure infections [6].

On reviewing literature, only few methods have been reported for the estimation of DLF including HPTLC [7], UHPLC/HPLC [8–10], and potentiometric methods [11]. However, some limitations could be debated for the techniques used in previously published methods. For instance, although UHPLC and HPLC techniques are recently widespread and became quite standard laboratory equipment, some socio-economic drawbacks could be disputed. First of all, HPLC & UHPLC systems are more expensive than spectroscopy equipment to purchase and maintain. The cost of columns, solvents, mobile phases and other consumables can also add up, especially for high sample throughput analysis [12]. Moreover, the utilization of high energy, and consumption of large amounts of organic solvents is less ecofriendly when compared to spectroscopic techniques [13]. Meanwhile, Potentiometric analysis has limited selectivity where it relies on the selective response of the electrode to a specific analyte. In the meantime, interferences from certain substances in sample matrices are common, besides the usual temperature change effects that could result in unwanted electromotive forces which affect their responses [14]. Potentiometric measurements also generally have higher detection limits compared to other analytical techniques such as spectrophotometry or chromatography. As will be demonstrated later, the proposed spectroscopic technique under study has at least 10 folds higher sensitivity compared to the published potentiometric technique [11].

Within the past 10 years, efficient probes have been developed using carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Their distinctive properties include high water solubility, health safety, unique photocatalytic activity, simplicity, and biocompatibility. Fluorescent probes are capable of interacting with the target substrate/analyte which results in a change in the resulting fluorescent output [15]. CQDs are ideal alternatives for the use of fluorescent dyes and luminous derivatization reagents because of their customizable optical features [16]. For the present time, transition metal nanostructures have gained great attention for their distinctive electro-optical properties. Doping carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with transition metals is a strategy used to modify their optical, electronic, and catalytic properties. Transition metals nanostructures also provides enhanced luminescent characters in semiconductor electro-luminescence as well as fluorescence devices [16]. This in turn improves their functions in treating impurities, and also increased the selectivity of their fluorescence of doped CQDs compared to N@CQDs [17].

Meanwhile, the implementation of green analytical chemistry (GAC) concepts focuses mainly on minimization or total removal of the use of dangerous chemical reagents and solvents. The development of novel analytical methods that are consistent with GAC principles can also be fitted in other ways such as decreasing the sample size, establishing uncomplicated straightforward sample preparation, and/or sensitive extraction methods. The management of analytical waste is another tool for GAC.

The history of use of quantum dots in the analysis of fluoroquinolones antibiotics is quite interesting. The choice of transition metal for doping carbon dots depends on the specific application requirements and desired properties. One of the most common and widely used QDs were those based on cadmium and telluride (CdTe) QDs. CdTe QDs were utilized in the quantification of 4 fluoroquinolones [18]. In 2014, Meng et al. [19], used CdTe QDs as a fluorescent background medium for analysis of 5 fluoroquinolones using micellar electrokinetic capillary chromatography. In 2020, a research group introduced the novel use of portable smart phones for onsite monitoring of environmental and food samples for their contents of fluoroquinolones using CdTe QDs [20]. CdTe based QDs were then furtherly improved, in 2023, using yttrium element for co-ordination which enhanced the sensitivity of such QDs in analysis of norfloxacin (LOD ≈ 30 nM) [21]. However, the previously mentioned QDs used in fluoroquinolones analysis were not eco-friendly. Cd is not a biodegradable element, which can accumulate in the environment, particularly in soil and water. Cd is a non-essential and toxic heavy metal, that has the potential to accumulate in human bodies with average half-life exceeding 19 years leading to human death [22]. Cd is also classified by the World Health Organization as group 1 carcinogen, with reported maximum permissible exposure limit not exceeding 1 μg daily [23]. Therefore, the analytical approaches linked to it is not favorable [24].

The proposed research highlights the possibilities of developing greener CQDs for monitoring fluoroquinolones. The capabilities of using Cu to replace those Cd doped QDs is being studied, which is much safer. Firstly, Copper is abundant in the Earth's crust, making it a relatively low-cost transition metal compared to others like platinum or gold. Copper is an essential micronutrient for many organisms, including plants, animals, and microorganisms, which plays a vital role in various biological processes with narrow toxicity range [25]. Moreover, copper possesses excellent catalytic properties, distinctive electro-optical properties, more stability, multiplexing ability, and high brightness [26]. The use of copper in the synthesis of carbon dots was introduced in many applications and found beneficial in fine-tuning the conduction band position of doped CQDs where its use leads to varying reaction mechanisms such as complexation [27], and artificial nano-enzyme catalysis [28]. Simultaneously, the use of greener sources of carbon urged the scientific community to report novel synthetic pathways. Therefore, in 2019, novel QDs were synthesized and reported based on natural carbon source, glutathione, however these were also based on doping with Cd and S for the analysis of fluoroquinolones [29]. Although the metal organic framework used in QDs synthesis has also benefitted of copper, as a safer natural element, for construction of a complex composite for sensing antibiotics in environmental water samples [30]. The developed complex composite QDs were also based on rhodamine B. Rhodamine B is a synthetic organic dye that belongs to the family of xanthene dyes which exhibits good fluorescent properties. However, rhodamine B has some serious dangers if accidently ingested besides its carcinogenicity and negative impact aquatic ecosystems [31]. Moreover, the developed QDs had a much more complex synthetic pathway. The green use of plum fruit for preparing carbon dots has shown to be very promising [32]. Plum fruits are naturally available worldwide at low cost. Their fruits are good source of phytoconstituents of anthocyanin, sugars, and phenolics [33]. The use of carbon dots produced from natural sources also proved to have the best efficiencies in biological applications [34].

In the proposed research, the use of copper and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu-N@CQDs) for coupling with DLF could be expected to promote the sensitivity and relative selectivity for the analyte quantification via hydrogen bond formation and electrostatic attractions. Nitrogen has a peculiar nature of their hydrogen bonds which has been found to be highly relevant for the detection of fluorine, through forming hydrogen bond interactions [35]. This covalent assembly has been also studied by several research groups for improving the selectivity and sensitivity of analytes’ detection using small fluorescent probes (for both in-vivo and in-vitro analyses) [15]. The interactions between Cu-N@CQDs and DLF could be hypothetically expected for the hydrogen bond neighbors to be established between nitrogen, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of Cu-N@CQDs from one hand, and the fluorine atoms of DLF on the other hand [17,35]. The presence of such hydroxyl, carboxyl and copper groups could be expected to improve the viability to conjugate with DLF [17,36]. Therefore, the aim of this work was to develop a new analytical methodology based on the use of a promising Cu-N@CQDs for the sensitive spectrofluorimetric quantitation of DLF. DLF could be effectively estimated when coupled with Cu-N@CQDs after excitation at 360 nm at emission wavelength of 440 nm (Fig. 1b).

Commercially available pure DLF was obtained from Melinta therapeutics (New Jersey, USA). Plum fruits were obtained from the Egyptian local market. Baxdela® tablets and vials (Labeled to contain 450 mg DLF per tablet, and 300 mg DLF per vial; Batch no. MEL 031-F001) were obtained from the local market. Copper sulphate and methanol were secured from the Al-Nasr Chemicals Co. (Cairo, Egypt). Human plasma samples were obtained from real serum pool of the Egyptian Blood Bank. Plasma samples were kept in freezer at −20 °C until analysis.

Britton–Robinson buffer was prepared by mixing equal volumes of 0.04 M H3BO3 (2.04 g/100 mL), 0.04 M H3PO4 (2.8 mL of 85 % H3PO4/100 mL), and 0.04 M CH3COOH (2.3 mL of glacial acetic acid/100 mL) that has been adjusted to the desired pH with 0.2 M NaOH [37].

For the catalysis of organic synthesis and solvent extraction, a microwave instrument (MFMI-100 A, from Medfuture, Jinan, China) was used. Philips X-ray diffractometer (model PW3040/00) was used for scanning the powder X-ray from Philips Innovation labs (Eindhoven, Netherlands). Metrohm pH meter 780 from Metrohm (Herisau, Switzerland). A JEM-100CXII high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) was used for capturing TEM images manufactured by JEOL (Tokyo, Japan). ZEN 3600 Zetasizer equipment from Malvern Instruments was used for particle sizing (range = 0.3–10000 nm) using dynamic light scattering technique (Malvern, UK).

Fluorescence spectrometer, model FS5, with 1 cm quartz sample cell and ozone-free xenon light source (150 W) was used along the experiment and was obtained from Edinburgh Instruments (Edinburgh, UK). FS5 had a 2 nm slit width, speed of scanning 1000 nm per second and data was collected using its supplied Fluoracle® software from Edinburgh Instruments (Edinburgh, UK). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) model FT-08 was purchased from Lumex instruments (Wakendorf, Germany). FT-IR spectra were recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 wave number via the KBr pellet procedure. During FTIR analysis of transmittance, samples were ground with potassium bromide (approximately 5 % of the sample weight) and pressed onto a rough pellet. The sample was then sandwiched between two transparent infrared plates using the transmission mode.

Cu-N@CQDs were synthesized by mixing plum juice (50 mL) together with copper sulphate (200 mg). The mixture was transferred to a reaction vessel and then placed for 5 min in the microwave (700 W, 2450 MHz) until a brownish solution was obtained. A sonication step was then followed for 20 min for the formed solution to remove large particles. A 0.45 μm cellulose membrane was used to filter the solution and then the filtrate was further centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 rpm. The supernatant was filtrated again through a 0.45 μm cellulose membrane filter. A yellow-colored solution was obtained which was finally used in the procedure.

Nitrogen-doped quantum dots (N@CQDs) were synthesized to be evaluated compared to the novel Cu-N@CQDs. This was performed using microwave assisted method [38]. An amount of 500 mg of sucrose and urea were dissolved in deionized water and heated under the microwaves (2450 MHz/700 W) for 8 min. The brown solution was then sonicated for 20 min before its filtration using 0.45 μm filters. The filtrate was furtherly centrifuged for 15 min at 5000 rpm and refiltered using 0.45 μm membrane filter.

A DLF standard solution was prepared at concentration of 50 μg mL−1 using authentic DLF powder dissolved in ultrapure water. To prepare working solutions, further serial dilutions were applied using distilled water. The working solutions were freshly prepared daily and could be stored in refrigerator at 4–8 °C up to 24 h [39].

A volume of 0.5 mL of the prepared 0.15 mg mL−1 Cu-N@CQDs was mixed with 1.0 mL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH 6.8). Then 1.0 mL of DLF working solution was added to obtain solutions into 10.0 mL volumetric flasks with final concentrations within the range (0.5–100.0 ng mL−1). The flasks were filled to volume using ultrapure water. The fluorescence intensity of each solution was measured at λ em 440 nm after excitation at 360 nm for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DLF determination in pharmaceutical dosage forms and content uniformity test
ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical tablet dosage forms were prepared by accurately weighing and then powdering ten Baxdela® 450 mg/tablets. An amount of Baxdela® powder, equivalent to 10 mg DLF, was then dissolved in 50 mL distilled water. After that, the mixture was sonicated for 6 min to complete drug extraction, followed by filtration. The filtrate was completed to a volume of 100 mL to obtain DLF solution at concentration 100 μg mL−1.

For the Baxdela® 300 mg vials, the contents of 5 vials were taken away, weighed accurately, and mixed. Another amount of the mix, equivalent to 10 mg DLF, was then dissolved in 100 mL distilled water. For the content uniformity test, uniformity testing was performed according to USP [40]. The procedure was carried out individually for each tablet in the pharmaceutical dosage form. The final solutions obtained from each test were furtherly diluted to be used in 1.0 mL aliquots according to the procedure described in subsection 2.5.

A 400 μL of the thawed plasma samples were spiked by different volumes of DLF standard solution (equivalent to concentration range between 0.05 and 10.00 μg mL−1 DLF) and placed into centrifugation test-tubes. For protein-precipitation, another 1.0 mL by volume of methanol was introduced. The test-tubes were vortexed for 30 s, then volume was completed to 10.0 mL with distilled water. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged for 30 min (at 3500 rpm), and then 1.0 mL of the clear supernatant was utilized in the procedure described in subsection 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of DLF in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk samples were spiked at different concentrations with DLF standard solution. One mL of methanol was mixed with 0.5 mL of each milk sample. The mixture was shaken for 30 s, then diluted to 10 mL with distilled water. Centrifugation was carried out at 3500 rpm for 10 min. One mL of the supernatant was then used for further examination [41] according to the procedure described in subsection 2.5. The same steps were followed for drug-free milk samples without the addition of DLF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability studies of the quantum dots as fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: By recycling test, DLF medication was calculated ten times (10 weeks) using Cu-N@CQDs to test the Cu-N@CQDs' reusability. After each cycle, the quantum dots were collected by centrifugation, cleaned with ultra-pure water and ethanol, and dried for 40 min at 50 °C so they could be reused. The quantum dots were stored at 4 °C for 10 weeks.

High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) was used to study the morphology of Cu-N@CQDs. Cu-N@CQDs exhibited nano-sized particles with an average diameter of about 2 nm as shown in Fig. 2 a. For more confirmation of the particle size, different samples were examined using TEM where the particle size was found to be ranged from 1.5 to 2.0 nm (Supplementary materials Fig. S1).

Another technique, dynamic light scattering (DLS), was used to confirm this average size using Zetasizer equipment (Fig. 2b). DLS approach showed a mean particle size of 2.4 ± 0.16 nm which agreed with TEM imaging. Fig. 2b shows an average size of 2.4 ± 0.16 nm which is slightly larger than TEM image where the larger molecules diffused slower than smaller molecules. Besides, the variations in the particle sizes could arise due to the random Brownian motion of the particles [42].

Fig. 2c shows a typical powder X-ray diffraction pattern (PXRD) of the resulting Cu-N@CQDs. Due to the tiny size of C-dots, a diffraction peak centered at 24.60° and 44.2° were developed, these peaks are associated with the and graphitization and amorphous carbons [28]. This was obtained due to the abundant surface functional groups and graphitization carbon [43]. Copper nanoparticles show distinct peaks at 43.75 (110) and 50.36 (200), which correspond to the face-centered-cubic (fcc) phase of copper, as well as a copper oxide Cu2O peak at 31.52 (111) [44]. The PXRD data supports the creation of a graphite-like structure and show the presence of Cu(II) in Cu-N@CQDs synthesized in a single step [45]. Other peaks could also be observed (Fig. 2c).

The functional groups for N@CQDs and Cu-N@CQDs nanoparticles were examined using their FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2d). Five distinctive peaks can be observed at 1441, 1572, 1637, 2975, and 3462 cm−1 that correspond to C-N, C-O, (OH, NH stretching), CO, and (NH, OH), respectively [28,46]. Moreover, the intensity of the peaks of N-CQDs are stronger compared to those of Cu-N@CQDs indicating formation of Cu-N@CQDs (Fig. 2d). The new peak developed at 618 cm−1 in Cu-N@CQDs spectrum corresponds to Cu-O, which can't be found in N@CQDs FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2d). Moreover, the decreasing peaks intensity of the FTIR spectrum indicates the interaction between DLF and Cu-N@CQDs due to hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction (Supplementary materials Fig. S2).

X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to study the elemental composition of Cu-N@CQDs. Four peaks could be observed at 932.11, 539.40, 394.91, and 285.80 eV. Those peaks correspond to Cu, O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, respectively (Fig. 3 a). The XPS of O 1s (Fig. 3b) identifies 2 characteristic peaks at 531.80 and 530.5 eV, which are correlated to C-O-C and Cu-O, respectively. Furthermore, another 4 peaks in C 1s spectrum at 284.5, 285.2, 286.4, and 288.5 eV were noticeable (Fig. 3c). Those are identifiable for the groups of (C-C and CC), C-N, C-O, and CO, respectively. The 2 peaks at 952.5 and 934.5 eV (Fig. 3d) are characteristic for Cu 2p1/2 of Cu+, and Cu 2p3/2 of Cu+2 [47].

Elemental analysis of Cu-N@CQDs has been also carried out. The Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum could be used to interpret Cu doping in N@CQDs (Supplementary materials Fig. S3a), where the peaks identifiable for C, N, O, and Cu could be observed. The appearance of different Cu L-lines at different eV in the EDX spectrum can be attributed to the interactions between the incident electron beam and Cu atoms in the developed QDs. The energy of the incident electrons determines the ionization and excitation processes that occur within the sample. Additionally, the detection system could have energy-dependent efficiency and resolution characteristics that can also influence the appearance of Cu peaks at different eV in the spectrum. Cu L-lines correspond to the emission of characteristic X-rays when inner-shell electrons in Cu are ionized and transitioned to lower energy levels [48,49]. The percentages of “C”, “N”, “O”, “H” and “Cu” present in the Cu-N@CQDs was found to be 44.30, 9.28, 27.82, 6.11 and 2.43 %, respectively (Supplementary materials Fig. S3a).

Meanwhile, the surface morphology of doped CQDs includes their surface topographical and structural features, which can be significantly influenced by the doping process. Doping can impact the surface roughness of CQDs, where rougher surfaces indicate the incorporation of dopant atoms. An image from the scanning electron microscope (SEM) showed the surface morphology of Cu-N@CQDs, with rough surface arrangement (Supplementary materials Fig. S3b).

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Cu-N@CQDs was calculated using the single point method as described in previously reported literatures [28,36,50], using quinine sulphate as reference. It has a 54 % QY in sulfuric acid solution (0.1 M) with 1.33 refractive index, While the quantum dots were measured in aqueous solution with refractive index equal to 1.

Where Q is the quantum yield and F refers to the integrated fluorescence.

A high QY of 40.20 % was calculated by the proposed method for amine Cu-N doped quantum dots. This can be attributed to the nano-sized Cu-N@CQDs (1.8 nm) which improved the QY and generate better optical effects on the quantum dots’ surface [51,52]. It worth mentioning that the fluorescence QY for N@CQDs was observed to increase when Cu was introduced as dopant. The QY for N@CQDs was 36.60 %, compared to the Cu-N@CQDs (40.20 %). Copper doping can lead to the formation of Cu-related defect states within the bandgap of the CQDs, which can significantly impact their optical and electronic properties. Cu dopants can act as luminescent centers, introducing additional radiative recombination pathways within the CQDs. The Cu-related defect states can facilitate efficient charge carrier trapping and recombination, leading to enhanced radiative recombination and, consequently, increased quantum yield [53,54].

UV spectra of Cu-N@CQDs provided 2 absorption peaks at 330 and 290 nm as shown in Fig. 4 a. Different wavelengths for excitation were tested within the range 290:410 nm for excitation-dependent emission of Cu-N@CQDs. As excitation wavelengths increased, it was observed that the emission spectra showed a red shift, which was followed by a decrease in the relative fluorescence intensity (RFI). This should confirm the carbon dots' excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 4b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of interaction between DLF and Cu-N@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: After synthesis and characterization, the developed Cu-N@CQDs were used for the determination of DLF. As seen in (Supplementary Material Fig. S4) the excitation and emission of DLF were observed at 290 nm and 501 nm respectively, which refers to no interference between native fluorescence of DLF and the reaction with quantum dots.

The surface morphology of Cu-N@CQDs contains various function groups as reported in Fig. 2d. Therefore, the fluorescence of Cu-N@CQDs is enhanced by conjugating them to DLF due to hydrogen bonding and electron donor-acceptor complex between Cu-N@CQD and ERV, as well as the presence of fragments carboxyl, hydroxyl and copper. The carboxyl or hydroxyl in the Cu-N@CQD group and ERV fluorine create strong and active local hydrogen bonds [36]. On the other hand, the carbon skeleton-stabilized electron co-acceptor representation of Cu-N@CQDs and the electron donor identity of DLF, can enhance the conjugation of CC bonds, as well as the coupling effect. The combination of hydrogen bonding and the action of the electron donor-acceptor complex gives rise to the appearance of giant fluorophores and chromophores [17,36].

Lifetime decay was studied for Cu-N@CQDs in absence and presence of DLF. The fluorescence lifetime curve as shown in (Supplementary Material Fig. S5 ) appears Cu-N@CQDs alone and Cu-N@CQDs with DLF appear at 7.02 and 8.31 ns, respectively, which confirms the interaction between Cu-N@CQDs and DLF, together with enhancement of the fluorescence [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method development and optimization using Cu-N@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The parameters affecting the method's sensitivity were then studied carefully and optimized to improve the responsiveness of DLF to Cu-N@CQDs. Those parameters include interaction time with DLF, the used buffer volume and pH, and concentration of Cu-N@CQDs. The optimization process had been studied using relative fluorescent intensity (RFI) as shown in Fig. 5 .

Buffer pH was studied within the range of 6.0–8.0 on the interaction of DLF with the fluorescent Cu-N@CQDs. The best fluorescent response was attained at pH 6.8 (Fig. 5a) using 1.0 ± 0.25 mL of BR buffer (Fig. 5b).

The concentration of Cu-N@CQDs was studied on the fluorescence interaction with DLF. Different Cu-N@CQDs concentrations at 0.1, 0.12, 0.13, 0.14, 0.15, 0.16, 0.17, 0.18, and 0.19 mg mL−1 were investigated (Fig. 5c). The results showed that a concentration of 0.15 mg mL−1 produced the best RFI.

The effect of interaction time effect was investigated up to 30 min (Fig. 5d). The ideal time was proven to be 10 min, where interaction was completed. Any further time was useless and didn't affect the RFI (Fig. 5d).

The microwave conditions for the quantum dots synthesis were also optimized. Microwave power was studied at 250, 500, 700 W. The maximum quantum yield was obtained at 700 W. In addition, the time of synthesis was examined at different times intervals (1–10 min). The maximum quantum yield was obtained from 5 to 8 min therefore 5 min was chosen as the best time for synthesis.

The proposed quantum dot method Cu-N@CQDs interaction with DLF was validated according to the ICH guidelines and bio-analytically validated via the United States Food and Drug Administration (US-FDA) guidelines [56,57].

A linear calibration curve was created (Supplementary materials Fig. S6) by plotting RFI obtained using spectrofluorimetry against DLF concentrations using 0.5 mL of the prepared 0.15 mg mL−1 Cu-N@CQDs (review procedure subsection 2.5). The calibration range was set at 0.5–100 ng mL−1 (Table 1 ). The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were determined by calculation using the slope and the standard deviation (ϭ) of the curve. LOQ was calculated as (10 ϭ/slope) and LOD as (3.3 ϭ/slope). The LOQ and LOD were found to be 0.27 and 0.08 ng mL−1 (Table 1) which indicates the high sensitivity of the proposed method.

The accuracy of the Cu-dots approach was examined using five different concentrations (10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, and 50.0 ng mL−1). The calculated percentage recovery results were within a range of 100.46 %–102.10 %, indicating high accuracy of the Cu-N@CQDs method (Table 2 ).

While the intra-day (repeatability) and inter-day precisions were studied using three different DLF concentrations at 20.0, 40.0, and 60.0 ng mL−1 using three measurements within the same day and at three different days. The results showed excellent precision of the proposed method (Table 2).

Robustness was evaluated using minor changes in the proposed methodology settings including Cu-N@CQDs concentration, the buffer volume & pH, and interaction time. Those minor modifications had a non-significant influence on the outcomes of recovery results of DLF determination (Table 3 ).

The plasma matrix effect was also studied on DLF determination. The results indicated the absence of any effect of the matrix within the studied range of DLF (Supplementary materials Table S7). The results obtained for the matrix effect have been compared to those obtained using N@CQDs. As shown in Supplementary materials Table S7, better percentage recovery results with higher precision were obtained.

DLF stability in human plasma was investigated at three quality control concentration levels, low (LQC 10 ng mL−1), medium (MQC 50 ng mL−1), and high (HQC 90 ng mL−1). A three freeze-thaw cycles (−24 °C), long-term stability (1 month at −24 °C), short-term stability (12 h at −24 °C), and post-preparative were studied. The recovery results (Supplementary materials Table S8 ) ranged between 96.52 and 98.73 %, indicating the high DLF stability in human plasma.

The Selectivity of Cu-N@CQDs was tested against other excipients such as talc powder, sucrose, mannitol, starch, magnesium stearate, and sodium chloride). As shown in supplementary materials Table S9, no interferences were observed between Cu-N@CQDs, DLF and excipients.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability studies of Cu-N@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The estimation of DLF 10 times (10 weeks), as shown in (Supplementary materials Figs. S10 and S11 ), was used to test the carbon dots' reusability and stability studies. The recycling investigation revealed no appreciable changes in the RFI system, demonstrating the carbon dots' excellent reusability for 10 cycles. Therefore, Cu-N@CQDs with strong reusability and stability indicated preferred long-term usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications in human plasma and milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to boost the applicability of the proposed nanoprobes, different matrices were examined for the quantification of DLF. DLF was spiked at different concentrations in drug-free human plasma samples as well as milk samples as biological matrices. Then, Cu-N@CQDs were used under the proposed analytical conditions for DLF quantification. The results (Table 4 ) show high recovery percentages of DLF using the proposed method from human plasma and milk, especially when compared to those obtained using N@CQDs (Table 4). This could be attributed to the fact that when copper was introduced into the carbon dots skeleton, it fine-tuned the conduction band position of doped CQDs. This in turn should have led to the adjustment of the functions and the fluorescence of doped carbon dots, and hence also increased their selectivity.

The proposed approach was used for the quantification of DLF in its commercial tablet and vial dosage forms (Baxdela® 450 mg/tablet and 300 mg/vial). The obtained percentage of recoveries ± RSD were discovered to be 102.10 ± 1.05 and 102.55 ± 0.96 in Baxdela® tablets and vials, respectively.

Content uniformity testing is another major approach in the quality control of pharmaceutical tablet dosage forms. The drug content of each manufactured tablet must fall falls within a specified range around the claimed labeled content. This in turn warrantee the uniformity of dosing in each unit [33,36]. Content uniformity testing could be a tedious and time-consuming process when complex approaches are followed. However, using Cu-N@CQDs for DLF quantification, the method was ideally suited with high throughput and cost-effectiveness. The uniformity test was applied according to the with USP guideline protocol [40]. Ten tablets were analyzed using the proposed Cu-N@CQDs approach. The acceptance value (AV) was then calculated. AV was calculated by multiplying the standard deviation of percentage recoveries obtained by 2.4, which was found to be lower than the permitted maximum acceptance value (L1) [40], indicating consistent batch production of DLF (Table 5 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Greenness evaluation of the proposed method versus the reported method
ce:section.ce:para: Several tools have been revealed for the assessment of the ecological impacts of analytical methods. Such assessment helps in attaining sustainability goals through minimizing the environmental harm of the routine analytical chemistry activities. For instance, a standard HPLC system is able to generate about 0.5 L of organic solvents as waste on average per day [1]. Therefore, the greenness assessment became a crucial step during novel methods’ evaluation. The proposed approach was compared to other previously reported chromatographic methods [8,10]. Two tools were utilized for such assessments, the green analytical procedure index (GAPI) [58] and AGREE [59].

The comparison details are shown in Table 6 . As shown, the proposed method can be used in determining DLF in pharmaceutical dosage forms, plasma samples, and milk compared to the other 2 chromatographic methodologies [8,10]. For greenness assessment, the proposed procedure used for human plasma preparation is more straightforward and less complicated, where it doesn't involve liquid-liquid extraction steps. The proposed method also avoids the use of acetonitrile an ecologically harmful solvent [1]. The energy consumption is higher for the previously reported methods [8,10] that use MS/MS detection, compared to the proposed instrumentation. The linearity range, and the results of LOD and LOQ (Table 6) are better for the proposed method indicating more sensitivity to the analyte under study. Moreover, the overall scores and color-coding of GAPI and AGREE pictograms show fewer red zones and hence superior ecological impact for the developed Cu-N@CQDs nanoprobes and fluorescent spectroscopy combined technique over the compared methodologies.

The proposed research tried to shed highlight on the possibilities of using Cu to replace those commonly used toxic transition elements, such as Cd, for doping CQDs, for the purpose of monitoring fluoroquinolones. In the proposed study, it was feasible to prepare Cu-N@CQDs from a green naturally occurring source, plum juice, compared to the previously reported use of toxic rhodamine B dye [30]. The use of plum juice also offers low-cost precursors and economical for large-scale synthesis with, as investigated, an improved quantum yield. Moreover, the proposed research used DLF as a fluoroquinolone model for the proposed useful QDs. DLF, as mentioned earlier, has been very recently approved, with few reported methodologies for its determination. This in turn offers a first to be reported use of fluorescence spectroscopy and QDs in its monitoring in different matrices including biological and pharmaceutical as well.

A newly established, straightforward, and cost-effective microwave-assisted synthesis approach was developed and validated for the synthesis of copper and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu-N@CQDs). The whole synthesis process consumed an average of 35 min for all steps including microwave thermolysis. The developed procedure resulted directly in manufacturing small-size and powerful fluorescence green Cu-N@CQDs with a high quantum yield (40.20 %). The chemical and optical stability of the prepared Cu and N-doped CDs were outstanding. These appealing features and advantages make the resulting M-CDs excellent platforms for fluorescent probes for the quantification of DLF in a variety of applications such as human plasma and milk samples (with a high percentage of recovery (99.12 %). Moreover, the greenness of this work was found to be in consistency with green analytical chemistry.
This research received no external funding.

Baher I. Salman: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ahmed I. Hassan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ahmed Al-Harrasi: Visualization, Supervision. Adel Ehab Ibrahim: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Data curation, Conceptualization. Roshdy E. Saraya: Validation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
